base_method_text:
{"arxiv_id":"2409.18374v1","arxiv_url":"http://arxiv.org/abs/2409.18374v1","title":"Adaptive Learning of the Latent Space of Wasserstein Generative
  Adversarial Networks","authors":["Yixuan Qiu","Qingyi Gao","Xiao Wang"],"published_date":"2024-09-27T01:25:22Z","journal":"","doi":"","summary":"Generative models based on latent variables, such as generative adversarial
networks (GANs) and variational auto-encoders (VAEs), have gained lots of
interests due to their impressive performance in many fields. However, many
data such as natural images usually do not populate the ambient Euclidean space
but instead reside in a lower-dimensional manifold. Thus an inappropriate
choice of the latent dimension fails to uncover the structure of the data,
possibly resulting in mismatch of latent representations and poor generative
qualities. Towards addressing these problems, we propose a novel framework
called the latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN) that fuses the Wasserstein
auto-encoder and the Wasserstein GAN so that the intrinsic dimension of the
data manifold can be adaptively learned by a modified informative latent
distribution. We prove that there exist an encoder network and a generator
network in such a way that the intrinsic dimension of the learned encoding
distribution is equal to the dimension of the data manifold. We theoretically
establish that our estimated intrinsic dimension is a consistent estimate of
the true dimension of the data manifold. Meanwhile, we provide an upper bound
on the generalization error of LWGAN, implying that we force the synthetic data
distribution to be similar to the real data distribution from a population
perspective. Comprehensive empirical experiments verify our framework and show
that LWGAN is able to identify the correct intrinsic dimension under several
scenarios, and simultaneously generate high-quality synthetic data by sampling
from the learned latent distribution.","github_url":"https://github.com/yixuan/LWGAN","main_contributions":"The paper introduces the Latent Wasserstein GAN (LWGAN), a novel framework that adaptively learns the intrinsic dimension of data distributions supported on manifolds. LWGAN fuses the Wasserstein Auto-encoder (WAE) and Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) to improve generative modeling quality and representation learning by identifying the intrinsic dimension of the data manifold. The authors theoretically prove the consistency and generalization error bounds of LWGAN.","methodology":"LWGAN combines WAE and WGAN. It uses a deterministic encoder to learn an informative prior distribution and a generator to produce realistic images from the latent code. The framework employs the 1-Wasserstein distance to measure the similarity between distributions and uses a rank-revealing matrix to characterize the intrinsic dimension of the latent space. The critic function's Lipschitz continuity is enforced via gradient penalty.","experimental_setup":"The framework's performance is validated on synthetic datasets (S-curve, Swiss roll, Hyperplane) and real image datasets (MNIST, CelebA). Intrinsic dimension estimation accuracy and the quality of generated samples are assessed. Metrics such as Inception Score (IS), Fréchet Inception Distance (FID), and reconstruction error are used for quantitative evaluation.","limitations":"The paper assumes the existence of a homeomorphism between the data manifold and a Euclidean space. The choice of neural network architecture and hyperparameters (e.g., gradient penalty, rank regularization) can significantly impact performance. The computational cost of training multiple GANs with varying latent dimensions could be high, although the paper proposes an efficient algorithm.","future_research_directions":"Future work includes investigating a more general scenario where the generator is stochastic by adding an extra noise vector to its input. Another direction involves incorporating the stochastic LWGAN into more recent GAN modules like BigGAN to produce high-resolution and high-fidelity images while estimating the intrinsic dimension. Applying LWGAN to structural estimation in econometrics is also mentioned."}
